name: Great Dane

dietaryNeeds.caloriesPerDay: 2500-4000+ kcal (adult); puppy carefully controlled to growth chart
dietaryNeeds.feedingAmount: Split across 2-3 meals; never one large meal (bloat risk)
keyNutrients: moderate fat to keep growth steady, not rapid
glucosamine/chondroitin and omega-3 for vulnerable joints
taurine and L-carnitine for the breed's high DCM risk
dietaryNeeds.breedSpecificNotes: Great Danes are the textbook giant breed: a puppy grows from ~600g to potentially 70kg+ over 18-24 months, and that staggering growth must be kept SLOW and STEADY, never fast. The single biggest dietary mistake is over-feeding or feeding a too-rich, high-calcium diet that pushes growth faster than the skeleton can safely build — the direct cause of developmental orthopaedic disease (HOD, OCD, panosteitis) and lifelong joint problems. They need a large/giant-breed puppy or all-life-stage food carrying the AAFCO 'including growth of large size dogs (70lb+ as an adult)' statement, controlled calcium (~1-1.2%) with a tight Ca:P ratio, and NO calcium supplements. As a deep-chested giant they are at the highest bloat (GDV) risk of any breed — feed 2-3 smaller meals from a floor-level bowl, never one large meal, and rest after eating. They mature late: stay on growth food until ~18-24 months, far longer than any medium or small breed.

bestOverall.brand: AVA Optimum Health Large Breed Puppy
bestOverall.why: A genuinely large/giant-breed-specific recipe with controlled calcium (1.5%) and energy density to keep a Dane's growth steady rather than rapid — exactly the priority for a giant pup — plus added glucosamine, chondroitin and omega-3 for vulnerable joints and a larger kibble that slows fast eaters.

bestForJoints.why: Cold-pressed and gentle on digestion with omega-3 for joint support, and the all-life-stage formulation avoids an awkward diet switch as a Dane matures over two slow years. Moderate fat keeps growth measured.

bestForSensitiveStomach.why: A single, gentle protein for a deep-chested breed where digestive upset and gas are concerns; feeding a highly digestible food in smaller meals helps reduce the bloating risk Danes are so prone to.

bestForWeightControl.why: Every meal is portioned to the dog's weight and target condition — invaluable for a giant breed where keeping a pup lean (body score ~4/9) is the single best protection against joint disease driven by over-fast growth.

bestPremium.why: Nutrient-dense, high named-meat recipes — but for a Dane choose the large-breed or all-life-stage line and watch calcium and calories carefully, because even premium food must keep a giant pup's growth slow and steady.

name: Dobermann
commonHealthIssues: dilated cardiomyopathy (DCM) — the highest-risk breed of all

dietaryNeeds.feedingAmount: 300-400g dry per day, split across 2 meals
keyNutrients: taurine and its precursors (methionine, cysteine) from meat-rich recipes
L-carnitine from red meat for heart-muscle energy
omega-3 (EPA/DHA) for cardiovascular and joint support
named animal protein over heavy legume loads
commonSensitivities: low-meat formulas that skimp on taurine precursors

dietaryNeeds.breedSpecificNotes: The Dobermann is the single breed most strongly associated with dilated cardiomyopathy (DCM) — studies put lifetime prevalence at well over 50%, the highest of any breed, and it is overwhelmingly GENETIC, not caused by diet. That said, nutrition is the one lever an owner can pull: feed a meat-rich diet that supplies taurine's building blocks (methionine and cysteine) and L-carnitine naturally, and steer clear of grain-free recipes that lean heavily on peas, lentils and chickpeas — the exact 'BEG' profile flagged in the FDA's DCM investigation. Diet does not prevent or cure genetic DCM, so this breed's owners should pair sensible feeding with periodic veterinary heart screening (echocardiography and Holter monitoring catch disease years before symptoms). As a lean, deep-chested athlete the Dobermann also needs higher fat to hold condition, and 2 smaller meals rather than one large bowl to reduce bloat risk.

bestOverall.why: Every Years recipe is grain- AND legume-free and formulated by a veterinary clinical nutrition specialist — which sidesteps the legume/'BEG' DCM concern entirely. For the breed with the highest DCM risk of all, that is exactly the profile to choose: meat-led nutrition without peas, lentils or chickpeas doing the heavy lifting.

bestForHeartHealth.why: 85% animal ingredients including organ meats (heart, liver) — the richest natural sources of taurine and L-carnitine, the two nutrients tied to canine heart-muscle function. A high-meat recipe lets a Dobermann make its own taurine from quality protein rather than relying on supplements.

bestForMuscle.why: An 80/20 meat-to-veg recipe with salmon for omega-3 — high named-meat content supports the lean, athletic musculature of a working Dobermann while the marine oils support the cardiovascular system.

bestForSensitiveStomach.why: 60% meat with sweet potato rather than a heavy pulse load for carbohydrate — a sensible heart-conscious profile that keeps meat firmly in the lead and pulses minor, British-made and traceable.

bestValue.why: Honest single-source meat at a fair price, gentle cold-pressed digestion and an all-life-stage formulation. Pair any value pick for this breed with periodic vet heart checks — diet supports the heart but genetics drive DCM.

name: Boxer
commonHealthIssues: arrhythmogenic right ventricular cardiomyopathy (ARVC) — 'Boxer cardiomyopathy', an inherited electrical heart disease
familial myocardial L-carnitine deficiency in some lines
Boxer colitis (histiocytic ulcerative colitis) and a sensitive digestion
very high lifetime cancer risk (mast cell tumours, lymphoma)
gastric dilatation-volvulus (bloat) in a deep-chested breed
brachycephalic tendency — prone to overheating and weight gain

dietaryNeeds.protein: 26-30% (high-quality named animal protein, red meat emphasised)
dietaryNeeds.fat: 14-18% (fuels a lean, athletic working dog without excess weight)
dietaryNeeds.feedingAmount: 280-380g dry per day, split across 2 meals
keyNutrients: L-carnitine from red meat — the Boxer's signature heart-energy nutrient
taurine and its precursors (methionine, cysteine) from meat-rich recipes
omega-3 (EPA/DHA) for cardiovascular and anti-inflammatory support
natural antioxidants given the breed's high cancer risk
named animal protein over heavy legume loads
commonSensitivities: rich or high-fat foods that upset the breed's notoriously sensitive gut
large single meals (bloat trigger in a deep-chested breed)
dietaryNeeds.breedSpecificNotes: The Boxer's heart story is genuinely its own. The breed's signature cardiac disease is not classic dilated cardiomyopathy but arrhythmogenic right ventricular cardiomyopathy (ARVC) — long known as 'Boxer cardiomyopathy' — an inherited electrical disease tied to a striatin gene mutation, where fibrofatty tissue replaces heart muscle and triggers dangerous rhythm disturbances (the classic sign is fainting). A commercial gene test and a 24-hour Holter monitor are the real screening tools; diet does not cause or cure ARVC. What makes the Boxer nutritionally distinct is L-carnitine: a documented family of Boxers had a myocardial L-carnitine deficiency (normal blood levels but low levels inside the heart muscle) and improved on supplementation. L-carnitine helps heart cells turn fat into energy and is found naturally in red meat — so a red-meat-rich, meat-led diet is the sensible everyday lever, while genetics and veterinary cardiac screening do the heavy lifting. Steer clear of legume-heavy grain-free 'BEG' recipes, support the heart with omega-3, lean on antioxidants given the breed's very high cancer rate, and respect a famously sensitive 'Boxer gut' (and Boxer colitis) by feeding gentle, named single proteins in two smaller meals rather than one large bowl. Any supplementation is a veterinary decision, not a DIY one.

bestOverall.why: Every Years recipe is grain- AND legume-free and formulated by a veterinary clinical nutrition specialist — which sidesteps the legume/'BEG' DCM concern entirely. For a heart-predisposed breed that also has a sensitive gut, meat-led nutrition without peas, lentils or chickpeas doing the heavy lifting is exactly the profile to choose.

bestForHeartHealth.why: 85% animal ingredients including red meat and organ meats (heart, liver) — the richest natural sources of L-carnitine and taurine, the two nutrients most tied to Boxer heart-muscle energy. For the breed where a familial myocardial L-carnitine deficiency is documented, a red-meat-rich recipe is the everyday lever (genetic testing and vet heart screening do the rest).

bestForMuscle.why: An 80/20 meat-to-veg recipe with salmon for omega-3 — high named-meat content supports the lean, powerful musculature of an athletic Boxer while the marine oils support the cardiovascular system and calm inflammation.

bestForSensitiveStomach.why: 60% meat with sweet potato rather than a heavy pulse load — a gentle, heart-conscious profile that suits the Boxer's notoriously delicate gut (and Boxer-colitis tendency), keeping meat firmly in the lead and pulses minor. British-made and traceable.

bestValue.why: Honest single-source meat at a fair price, gentle cold-pressed digestion that sits well with a sensitive Boxer gut, and an all-life-stage formulation. Pair any value pick for this breed with ARVC gene testing and periodic vet heart checks — diet supports the heart but genetics drive the disease.
